book_claims.claim: Traditional Mediterranean diet included abundant wild herbs and greens at every meal, including dandelions, plantain, watercress, mallow, and purslane
book_claims.mechanism: Wild edible plants provide a broader spectrum of phytochemicals, higher nutrient density, and more diverse fiber types than cultivated varieties

book_claims.claim: Provide antioxidants including vitamin C, vitamin E, beta-carotene, and selenium that protect against oxidative damage driving chronic diseases
book_claims.mechanism: Antioxidants neutralise free radicals and prevent LDL oxidation that drives atherosclerotic plaque formation

supplementary_research.finding: Ethnobotanical review of wild food plants in the Mediterranean: traditional diets included 200+ wild species. Many wild greens contain 2-10x the polyphenol and antioxidant content of cultivated counterparts.

Wild edible plants from Mediterranean regions showed significantly higher antioxidant capacity, total phenolics, and flavonoids compared to commercial cultivated vegetables. Dandelion greens had 3x the polyphenols of cultivated lettuce.

Comprehensive review of wild food plants used in traditional Italian cuisine. Over 700 species documented. Many have both nutritional and medicinal properties reflecting the original meaning of 'pharma-food'.

texture: varied: tender (mallow, lamb's quarters) to crunchy (purslane stems)
often more fibrous than cultivated greens

culinary_pairings.source: Traditional dressing for wild greens; fat enhances absorption of fat-soluble phytochemicals
Acid tames bitterness of wild greens; enhances iron absorption
Sauteed wild greens with garlic and olive oil is the archetype of cucina povera
Wild greens and cannellini beans is a traditional Tuscan staple
Wild green frittata or omelette; fat from yolk aids nutrient absorption
Acidic dressing for bitter wild greens; traditional preservation method
Umami balances bitterness; traditional combination in Southern Italian cooking

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: 200-800 mg depending on species (2-10x cultivated greens)
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Wild plants produce more polyphenols as defense chemicals. Many are metabolized by gut bacteria into bioactive compounds.
High bioavailability. Wild greens often exceed cultivated varieties. Rapidly destroyed by cooking.
Fat-soluble. Wild dandelion greens contain more beta-carotene than carrots.
Purslane is the richest leafy source. Most wild greens contain more ALA than cultivated lettuce.

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: Variable; high in dandelion root and chicory
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Prebiotic fiber that selectively feeds Bifidobacteria. Not digested by human enzymes.
